text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, Denmark Hill, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Harris, DOB: 14-03-1989, Hospital No: KCH-782311 NHS No. 9432015876  
12 Brookside Close, London, SE15 3QT

Thank you for your referral. I reviewed Daniel Harris following concerns raised by his employer about recurrent collapse events occurring during shifts in a commercial kitchen. He works the hot line in a high-temperature, fast-paced environment, often during evening service. He attended today with his colleague who has witnessed several recent episodes.

According to the colleague, the typical event begins without warning while he is plating or working over the grill. He becomes unresponsive, stares ahead, and his posture slackens; there may be low, indistinct vocal sounds. On two occasions he slid down a stainless-steel unit and briefly rocked at the shoulders. There has been no clearly observed rhythmic limb jerking, tongue biting, or incontinence reported. He appears confused after the episode and needs 30–60 minutes before he can resume tasks, often describing a heavy head and overwhelming fatigue. He does not reliably recall the event itself.

Daniel Harris reports that these episodes crop up most shifts, especially during the busiest part of service, and his manager has had to reassign him from open flame stations as a precaution. He also notes that, on getting home, he sometimes wakes disoriented on the sofa with no memory of lying down, suggesting possible unwitnessed events outside work as well. Heat, noise, and pressure to keep pace may be contributory triggers from his perspective.

Medication history: he has recently restarted levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily after a lapse in adherence due to rota changes and missed GP appointments; he believes that when he takes treatment consistently, the kitchen events thin out to only the odd occurrence across a handful of shifts. He denies alcohol misuse; he drinks coffee during service and little fluid otherwise. Sleep is fragmented due to alternating late finishes and early prep starts.

Examination today was unremarkable. Neurological assessment between episodes showed normal cranial nerves, full strength, intact coordination, and no focal deficits. Cardiovascular observations were stable with no orthostatic drop. There were no features to suggest an active intercurrent illness. Collateral descriptions are limited by the hectic nature of the kitchen and the lack of continuous observation.

Given the ongoing events arising on most working days and the safety-critical environment (sharp tools, hot oil, open flames), further characterisation is necessary. I have requested urgent ambulatory EEG with concurrent event diary, and I will also arrange a sleep-deprived EEG if the first study is non-diagnostic. Basic bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile, glucose, magnesium) and serum antiseizure medication level have been ordered. I have advised the employer and patient that a temporary restriction from high-risk stations is prudent pending assessment, and the patient will discuss adjusted duties with occupational health. We also talked about hydration and structured meal breaks during service, as he reports going long stretches without fluids.

Daniel Harris understands the plan and agrees to keep a detailed log of events, noting timing, context (heat level, task, stress), and recovery period. I will review him in clinic after investigations. Please let me know if there is any additional history from primary care that would assist, particularly previous imaging or prior EEG results.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: ongoing events arising on most working days

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes current events as occurring on most working days and most shifts, with possible additional unwitnessed events at home. There is no explicit numeric count per day/week, but the phrasing indicates frequent, recurring episodes across the week. Since the exact number is not specified and cannot be converted to a precise daily rate, the most appropriate normalized label is 'multiple per week.' This reflects 'most working days' without over-specifying. No evidence supports seizure freedom.
seizure_frequency_number: ongoing events arising on most working days

flaws.description: Gold (multiple per week) disagrees with Reference (1 per day)
